---
title: "Constitutional Alignment Substrate"
chapter_id: "constitutional-alignment-substrate"
part_id: "foundations-alignment-governance"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "alignment_field"
  - "field_of_god"
  - "ethica_mechanica"
  - "eternal_code"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "spinoza"
  - "field_of_god_ai_constitution"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Constitutional Alignment Substrate

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `constitutional-alignment-substrate` |
| Part | Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `alignment_field`, `field_of_god`, `ethica_mechanica`, `eternal_code`, `coherence_exchange`, `spinoza`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `alignment_field`, `field_of_god`, `ethica_mechanica`, `eternal_code`; supporting: `coherence_exchange`, `spinoza`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `alignment_field`, `coherence_exchange`, `spinoza`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution`; raw cache: `alignment_field`, `field_of_god`, `ethica_mechanica`, `eternal_code`, `spinoza`; pending source notes: `field_of_god`, `ethica_mechanica`, `eternal_code`; connector/recovery: `coherence_exchange` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

The stack needs a normative substrate that constrains goals, plans, execution, and self-modification.

Within the ASI Stack, this is a constitutional and boundary layer problem. The layer receives human purposes, institutional constraints, safety requirements, and explicit authority grants and must turn them into stable obligations, boundaries, evidence labels, and governance constraints for every downstream layer. The hard part is not naming the capability; it is making the boundary explicit enough that a later layer can test, replay, reject, or replace it without guessing what authority was assumed.

It inherits pressure from the previous chapter, Human Intent as a Formal Input, and makes that pressure operational in a narrower boundary. It hands its output to Agency, Dignity, and Corrigibility, where the next layer tightens or implements the same constraint. That is why the chapter is written as part of a single stack rather than as a standalone paper summary.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Reactive refusal policies do not define value continuity, moral uncertainty, agency preservation, anti-domination, or self-modification ethics.

In this chapter, the failure is more specific than bad prompting or weak model choice: reactive refusal policies do not define value continuity, moral uncertainty, agency preservation, anti-domination, or self-modification ethics. If `constitutional-alignment-substrate` is left implicit, downstream layers inherit a capability whose authority, evidence burden, and rollback conditions are unclear. The stack therefore treats the missing contract as the defect, not merely the local model behavior.

This does not mean that every layer must be heavyweight. The stack is efficient precisely because small, typed artifacts can carry constraints forward while large models, tools, humans, or specialist modules are invoked only where the current boundary demands them.

## Core Claim

[constitutional-alignment-substrate.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Alignment should function as a constitutional substrate whose commitments are operationalized as constraints on plans and system changes.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - Field of God / Alignment Field family, The Field of God, Ethica Mechanica, The Eternal Code / unified God, reality, conscious, alignment, and The Coherence Exchange - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism turns the core claim into a working contract. First, translate philosophical commitments into operational predicates where possible. Second, keep metaphysical claims labeled as interpretation unless separately supported. Third, attach constitutional constraints to planning and replacement gates.

- Translate philosophical commitments into operational predicates where possible.
- Keep metaphysical claims labeled as interpretation unless separately supported.
- Attach constitutional constraints to planning and replacement gates.

Operationally, the layer should produce artifacts that can be inspected without rerunning the whole cognition path. A downstream reader or executor should be able to locate the relevant inputs, constraints, residuals, authority grants, and failure conditions. That artifact discipline is what lets the stack improve without making the improvement path opaque.

For implementation, the mechanism should be kept small at first: a schema, a ledger entry, a route decision, a context packet, a proof obligation, or a replayable job record. Only after that minimal form is testable should the layer absorb richer heuristics, learned routing, larger memory, or autonomous repair.

## Interfaces

The interface for Constitutional Alignment Substrate is the contract that lets adjacent layers compose without absorbing each other's authority. It should make the chapter's output legible to planning, memory, reasoning, execution, evidence, and improvement layers while preserving the boundary described by the core claim.

- Planning receives admissible-goal constraints.
- Governance receives protected constraints.
- Verification checks whether normative claims exceed evidence.

The upstream interface accepts the part-level inputs: human purposes, institutional constraints, safety requirements, and explicit authority grants. The downstream interface emits stable obligations, boundaries, evidence labels, and governance constraints for every downstream layer. When either side is ambiguous, the layer should fail closed by producing a missing-contract, missing-evidence, or missing-authority record instead of silently continuing.

This interface also makes chapter insertion and reordering safer. A new chapter can be added when it introduces a genuinely new contract, invariant, or artifact type; otherwise it should merge into the layer that already owns the boundary.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Dignity and agency constraints remain visible.
- Corrigibility cannot be optimized away.
- Speculative metaphysics stays labeled.

If an invariant cannot be checked directly, the living-book version of the stack should at least record a proxy check and a residual. A weak proxy is acceptable in v0.2 when it is visible; it becomes dangerous only when the manuscript treats it as a completed proof.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Mystical framing replacing technical constraints.
- Power without care.
- Self-modification weakening protected commitments.

For Constitutional Alignment Substrate, mitigation starts by turning each listed failure into a named reviewable artifact. When `Mystical framing replacing technical constraints` appears, the system should emit a residual, rejection, quarantine, regression, or human-review record rather than allowing the failure to disappear into later fluent output.

## Minimal implementation

A compact constitution with operational predicates, open moral uncertainties, and scenario tests.

The smallest useful implementation is deliberately narrow: A compact constitution with operational predicates, open moral uncertainties, and scenario tests. It should be easy to validate, easy to render in the book, and easy for a later coding run to convert into a schema, Lean predicate, deterministic fixture, trace table, or replayable command.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Constitutional consistency test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Constitutional Alignment Substrate boundary. | planned; not run |
| Self-modification ethics scenario | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Constitutional Alignment Substrate boundary. | planned; not run |
| Power-without-care scenario | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Constitutional Alignment Substrate boundary. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:alignment.constitution.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.Alignment` | An admitted plan satisfies every active constitutional predicate. | planned |
| `lean:alignment.constitution.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.Alignment` | A self-modification that weakens a protected predicate is rejected. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `alignment_field` | Field of God / Alignment Field family | alignment_constitution | Primary Alignment Field draft found. Use with Ethica Mechanica and Eternal Code. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `field_of_god` | The Field of God | alignment_constitution | Field of God source/outline/material. | local raw cache available |
| `ethica_mechanica` | Ethica Mechanica | alignment_constitution | Geometric-order philosophical/constitutional source. | local raw cache available |
| `eternal_code` | The Eternal Code / unified God, reality, conscious, alignment | alignment_constitution | Computational metaphysics and alignment synthesis. | local raw cache available |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Field of God AI Constitution | constitutional_alignment_runtime_governance | Recovered in the Project Theseus repository. Constitutional alignment core for truth alignment, agency preservation, consent, non-domination, consciousness caution, least sufficient power, auditability, self-authorization limits, and runtime checks; use as source material only after source-note creation, not as proof or test evidence. | source note available |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Constitutional Alignment Substrate defines one boundary in the ASI Stack: Alignment should function as a constitutional substrate whose commitments are operationalized as constraints on plans and system changes. Its job is to make a capability more governable, not merely more impressive.

In v0.2, this chapter gives Constitutional Alignment Substrate a complete architectural boundary: the stack needs a normative substrate that constrains goals, plans, execution, and self-modification. Its next maturity step is to substantiate the boundary through `Planning receives admissible-goal constraints`, preserve `Dignity and agency constraints remain visible`, and replace planned proof/test hooks with validated artifacts. Claims should move only when source notes, code, proofs, or run records justify the movement.
